title: The Pinch Runner: When to Deploy Your Speed

root.narration: Bottom of the 7th, down 4-3 with your slow-footed cleanup hitter on first and one out. Your fastest player is on the bench, available as a pinch runner -- he's your best base-stealer with 90th-percentile sprint speed. But here's the thing: once you use him as a pinch runner, he's in the game and your cleanup hitter is done for the day. The 8th and 9th innings might need a big bat. And if this rally fizzles, you've burned your best speed weapon. Do you deploy the pinch runner now, or save him?

choices.text: Put in the pinch runner NOW. We need this run and a slow runner on first limits our options. Speed opens up the steal, avoids the double play, and scores from first on a double.
Save the pinch runner. We might need him more in the 8th or 9th with a closer situation. The cleanup hitter might come up again too -- his bat is worth more than a pinch runner's speed right now.
Wait one batter. If the next hitter gets on, the rally is real and I'll put in the pinch runner. If the next hitter makes an out, I'll save the speed for later.

decision-pinch-now-2.narration: Your speed demon enters the game at first base. He takes an aggressive lead. The pitcher is clearly uncomfortable -- he throws over to first twice. The next batter steps in. With your fast runner on first, you have options that didn't exist before. The pitcher is distracted. What's the play?

choices.text: Steal second! He's got the speed to swipe it easily. Put the tying run in scoring position without needing a hit.
Hit-and-run. The runner breaks and the hitter puts the ball in play. Opens holes and the runner's speed gives him an extra base on contact.
Just let the hitter hit. The speed is in the game -- it'll help on any ball in play. No need to get fancy.

decision-save-2.narration: You keep the slow cleanup hitter on first. The next batter hits a ground ball to the shortstop -- the shortstop flips to second for the force on your slow runner. The relay goes to first... your batter beats it out! No double play, but your cleanup hitter is out of the game anyway -- he was forced at second. Now there's a runner on first, two outs, and neither your pinch runner NOR your cleanup hitter is in the game for the rest of the day. You saved the pinch runner but lost the cleanup hitter to a fielder's choice.

choices.text: NOW put in the pinch runner for the guy on first. We still have a chance this inning -- speed on the bases with two outs might matter.
Still save the pinch runner. Two outs, runner on first -- this inning probably isn't going to produce. Save the speed for a better spot.

decision-wait-2.narration: You wait one batter. The next hitter singles to right! Now you have runners on first and second, one out -- the rally is real! Your slow cleanup hitter is lumbering around first and heading to second. He makes it to second safely, but barely. Now do you put in the pinch runner? The tying run is on second base with a slow runner.

choices.text: YES! Put the pinch runner in at second base. The tying run is 180 feet from home with a slow runner -- I need speed to score on a single.
Keep the cleanup hitter at second. He can still score on a double or an extra-base hit. Save the pinch runner.

outcome-pinch-steal-great.narration: Your pinch runner gets a great jump and steals second standing up! The catcher's throw isn't even close. Tying run in scoring position, one out, no hit needed. The next hitter lines a single to center -- your pinch runner rounds third at full speed and scores easily! TIE GAME! A slower runner might have been held at third on that single. The pinch runner's speed turned a single into a go-ahead run. THAT is why you deploy speed early: it creates runs that wouldn't exist with a slow runner.

outcome.headline: Pinch Runner Steals Second and Scores! Game Tied!
outcome.explanation: The pinch runner changed the game in two ways: 1) He stole second base without needing a hit, putting the tying run in scoring position for free. 2) His speed allowed him to score from second on a single that would have held a slower runner at third. That's TWO bases of value from speed alone. The timing was right because: the rally was happening NOW (runner on first, one out, down one), the cleanup hitter was slow (limiting offensive options), and the game was late enough that creating a run in the present is worth more than preserving a bench option for the future. The rule of thumb: deploy your pinch runner when the run matters most and the rally is live.
outcome.whatToRemember: Deploy pinch runners when the rally is live and the run matters NOW. Speed creates runs that wouldn't exist with slow runners -- stolen bases move runners without hits, and sprint speed turns singles into scoring plays.

keyTerms.definition: A player's top running speed measured in feet per second. The MLB average is 27 ft/s. Elite runners reach 30+ ft/s, which translates to roughly 0.3 seconds faster on a 90-foot base path.
The stolen base success rate needed to add offensive value. At approximately 70% success rate, stealing breaks even. Above 80%, it adds significant value.
The strategic decision of when to insert a speed specialist into the game. Best deployed when a live rally exists, the runner on base is slow, and the game situation demands immediate run creation.

outcome-pinch-hitrun-good.narration: Hit-and-run is on! The pinch runner breaks with the pitch, and the hitter grounds it to the right side. The second baseman fields it but the runner is already past second -- he rounds the bag and the second baseman has no play at second. He throws to first for the out. Two outs, pinch runner on second. The next hitter drives a single to center and the pinch runner scores from second easily! Tie game! The hit-and-run got the fast runner to second, and his speed scored him on a single.

outcome.headline: Tying Run Scores! Speed Makes the Difference!
outcome.explanation: The hit-and-run worked because the pinch runner's speed turned a force-out-at-second into a runner-at-second. A slower runner would have been forced at second on the ground ball, leaving nobody in scoring position. The fast runner was already past second base by the time the fielder got the ball, so the defense could only get the out at first. Then his speed scored him from second on a single -- a slower runner might have been held at third. The pinch runner produced two extra bases of value on this sequence through pure speed.
outcome.whatToRemember: Speed on the bases changes the math on hit-and-run plays. A fast runner turns fielder's choices into runner-in-scoring-position situations because the defense can't get the force at second base.

outcome-pinch-swing-good.narration: You let the hitter swing away. He drives a double into the left-center gap! The pinch runner, with his elite speed, rounds second and races home -- he scores standing up! TIE GAME! A slower runner would have been held at third on that double -- the outfielder had a strong arm and the throw came in to the cutoff man. But the pinch runner's speed was so overwhelming that the third base coach didn't even hesitate: he waved him home. The speed turned a double-and-runner-on-third into a double-and-tying-run-scores.

outcome.headline: Pinch Runner Scores from First on a Double! Game Tied!
outcome.explanation: Even without a steal or hit-and-run, the pinch runner's value was clear: he scored from first on a double that would have held a slower runner at third. That's the difference between tying the game now and needing another hit to tie it. Speed has hidden value that doesn't show up on the stat sheet -- it turns 'runner at third, one out' into 'run scores, runner at second, one out.' Over a full game, these extra bases add up to extra runs. The pinch runner decision was correct because the speed created a run that wouldn't have existed otherwise.
outcome.whatToRemember: Pinch runners add value even without steals or hit-and-runs. Their speed allows them to take extra bases on hits, score from first on doubles, and score from second on singles that would hold slower runners.

outcome-save-late-pr-okay.narration: You put in the pinch runner at first with two outs. He takes a lead, but the situation is worse than before: two outs, runner on first, down one. The next hitter flies out to center. Inning over. The pinch runner never got to use his speed. You saved him too long and the opportunity passed. The best chance to use the pinch runner was when there were runners in scoring position with fewer outs, not with two outs and the runner on first.

outcome.headline: Wasted Pinch Runner. Too Late to Matter.
outcome.explanation: Deploying the pinch runner with two outs and a runner on first was too late. The optimal time was with one out and a runner on first -- that's when speed has the most leverage. With one out, the pinch runner could have stolen second (putting the tying run in scoring position), avoided a double play on a ground ball, or scored from first on an extra-base hit. With two outs, those options are reduced -- there's no double play to worry about, and the runner needs a hit to score regardless of speed. By saving the pinch runner too long, you missed the window where speed mattered most.
outcome.whatToRemember: Pinch runners are most valuable with fewer outs and runners in scoring position or close to scoring position. With two outs and a runner on first, speed has less leverage. Deploy speed early in the rally, not after the best opportunities have passed.

outcome-save-keep-saving-okay.narration: You save the pinch runner. The inning ends with a strikeout. In the 8th, you get a runner on second with two outs -- and NOW you wish you had the pinch runner in the game, but the opportunity to insert him doesn't exist because there's nobody on base to replace. In the 9th, you get a pinch-hit opportunity and use your speed player as a pinch hitter -- he weakly grounds out. You lost 4-3. The pinch runner was never deployed in the role where he's most valuable: running the bases.

outcome.headline: Never Used the Pinch Runner. Opportunity Wasted.
outcome.explanation: The most common pinch-runner mistake is saving him for the 'perfect' moment that never comes. The 7th inning with a runner on first and one out WAS the moment. A live rally with the tying run on base in the late innings is exactly when speed has maximum value. By saving the pinch runner, you: 1) Kept a slow runner on first who limited your offensive options. 2) Lost the cleanup hitter to a fielder's choice anyway. 3) Never found a better opportunity to use the speed. The lesson: don't save roster moves for hypothetical future scenarios when a concrete opportunity exists right now. The bird in hand beats the one in the bush.
outcome.whatToRemember: Use your pinch runner when the rally is happening, not when you hope a rally might happen later. A concrete opportunity in the 7th is worth more than a hypothetical one in the 9th.

outcome-wait-pinch-great.narration: You put the pinch runner in at second base. Smart -- the rally developed, so now you deploy the speed. The next hitter drives a single to right field. The pinch runner, with elite speed, rounds third and races home. The right fielder throws... the runner beats it by three steps! TIE GAME! A slower runner is held at third on that single. The pinch runner's speed scored the tying run on a play that isn't even close. You waited for the rally to develop, confirmed it was real, and then deployed your weapon at the perfect moment.

outcome.headline: Pinch Runner Scores! Perfect Timing on the Deploy!
outcome.explanation: Waiting one batter before deploying the pinch runner was the optimal strategy here. By waiting, you: 1) Confirmed the rally was real (the next hitter singled). 2) Deployed speed at a higher-leverage position (second base instead of first). 3) The pinch runner at second only needs a single to score, whereas from first he needed a double or a steal plus a single. The wait cost you nothing because the slow runner reached second safely on the single. The risk of waiting is that the next batter makes an out and the rally dies -- but the reward is deploying your speed in a higher-leverage position. When you're uncertain, waiting one batter to see if the rally develops is a smart compromise between deploying too early and too late.
outcome.whatToRemember: Waiting one batter to deploy a pinch runner can be optimal when the rally isn't confirmed yet. If the rally develops, you deploy speed at a better position. If it dies, you saved your weapon.

keyTerms.definition: Waiting for evidence that an offensive rally is real (additional baserunners) before deploying limited bench resources like pinch runners. Prevents wasting assets on false rallies.
A runner on second base has higher scoring probability than first base. Deploying a pinch runner at second rather than first means speed is applied at a higher-value position.
Strategic deployment of bench players (pinch hitters, pinch runners) to maximize their impact. Requires balancing immediate needs against future potential needs.

outcome-wait-keep-okay.narration: You keep the slow cleanup hitter at second. The next batter singles to center. The cleanup hitter rounds third but he's slow -- the center fielder charges and throws home. The catcher gets the ball and the slow runner is... OUT at the plate! He was out by two full steps. The tying run was right there, 90 feet away, and your slow runner couldn't beat the throw. The pinch runner scores that play standing up. You had the perfect opportunity and didn't use your best weapon.

outcome.headline: OUT AT HOME! Slow Runner Can't Score on a Single!
outcome.explanation: This is the exact scenario that pinch runners exist for. The tying run was on second base, a single was hit, and the runner was thrown out at home because he was too slow. The pinch runner -- with 90th-percentile sprint speed -- scores that play easily. You had the asset on the bench, you had the perfect deployment opportunity (tying run on second in a live rally), and you chose not to use it. The cleanup hitter's future at-bats (which you were saving him for) are now worthless because he's OUT at home and the game continued without the tying run scoring. Saving bench assets for the future only makes sense if the future opportunity is BETTER than the present one. This was an A+ opportunity.
outcome.whatToRemember: When the tying run is on second in a live rally and you have a pinch runner available, deploy the speed. A slow runner thrown out at the plate is the worst possible use of your bench -- the run was right there and speed would have scored it.
